topic: SAA formation ~950 AD (matches Satan's little season ~1000 AD)
our_argument_summary: Our review says the ~950 AD date for SAA formation comes from published paleomagnetic literature (Campuzano 2019 SHAWQ2k, Tarduno 2015, Hare 2018), not from dome geometry. Earlier SAA-like episodes (~400-700 AD) are LLSVP-driven recurrences. The dome adopted the published date and noted proximity to ~1000 AD (Revelation 20 'Satan's little season'). Verdict: Unfalsifiable — theological overlay on a scientific observation without dome-derived mechanism.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-023 is a static entry in inject_ai_layer.py's confirmed_wins array with data_source='Paleomagnetic', year='Historical', status='confirmed'. Zero calculation. The ~950 AD date is declared, not derived.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded. No formula connects dome parameters (disc_radius, firmament_height, etc.) to a date. The date is adopted from external paleomagnetic literature.
data_sources_used: None fetched by the dome's automation. The date comes from Campuzano et al. 2019 (SHAWQ2k paleomagnetic reconstruction).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — this is a static historical claim with no monitoring or adaptive logic.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly identifies this as a hardcoded declaration with no dome-derived computation.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-023 appears only as a static entry in inject_ai_layer.py's confirmed_wins array. No domain in monitor.py fetches paleomagnetic data or validates the ~950 AD claim. The 'monitoring' is a static declaration of 'confirmed' status.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The dome model doesn't rename the standard geophysics explanation (LLSVP-driven flux expulsion) — it adds an entirely separate theological interpretation (Revelation 20 timeline). This is an unfalsifiable overlay, not a vocabulary substitution. Standard physics says 'core-mantle boundary dynamics'; the dome doesn't call this 'aetheric SAA formation' — it calls it a theological marker.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The ~950 AD date comes from Campuzano et al. 2019 (SHAWQ2k), published well before the dome model incorporated it. Tarduno et al. 2015 and Hare et al. 2018 also predate the dome model's adoption of this claim. The dome found the date in published literature and declared it a confirmed prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula or derivation path connects dome geometry (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, etc.) to the date ~950 AD. The dome model provides no mechanism for why the SAA should form at any particular time. The date is simply adopted from paleomagnetic literature.

cycle_2_comparison.changes_since_cycle_1: All three Cycle 1 issues were addressed: (1) CHAOS-7 citation replaced with proper paleomagnetic sources (Campuzano, Tarduno, Hare), (2) date range clarified to distinguish recurrence episodes from current SAA formation, (3) retrodiction framing sharpened with explicit 'retrodiction dressed as prediction' language. The text was substantially rewritten — detail_evidence grew from ~600 chars (estimated Cycle 1) to 1063 chars.

holes_found.description: FINDING FIELD OVERLONG FOR SUMMARY TABLE: The 'finding' field is 269 characters — a full paragraph with parenthetical citations and semicolons. Most other WINs have concise one-liners in the 50-120 character range. In the summary table, this will visually dominate its row and may wrap awkwardly. A dome defender scanning the table might see the verbosity as compensatory — as if we need more words because the argument is weaker.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the finding to a one-liner, e.g.: 'Real paleomagnetic date (Campuzano 2019) adopted as dome "prediction" — theological overlay without dome-derived mechanism.' Move the nuance about LLSVP recurrences to the detail block where it already lives.

holes_found.description: HARE ET AL. 2018 DOI DISCREPANCY: The detail_evidence links to DOI 10.1073/pnas.1720404115 for Hare et al. 2018. The Cycle 1 review recommended DOI 10.1073/pnas.1809197115. These are different DOIs. Without live verification, one may be incorrect. If the wrong DOI resolves to a different paper, a defender could flag a citation error — the exact kind of sloppiness we criticize in the dome model.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify which DOI is correct for Hare et al. 2018 'New archeomagnetic directional records from Iron Age southern Africa (ca. 425–1550 CE) and implications for the South Atlantic Anomaly' in PNAS 115(52). The correct DOI should be confirmed via DOI resolution before the next build.

holes_found.description: STATISTICAL RECURRENCE ARGUMENT UNDEREXPLOITED: The detail_evidence mentions earlier SAA episodes (~400-700 AD) as LLSVP-driven recurrences, which is good. But it doesn't quantify why the ~950 AD / ~1000 AD 'match' is statistically unimpressive. With recurrence intervals of ~200-400 years (Hare et al.), and archaeomagnetic dating uncertainty of ±50-100 years, the probability of some episode falling within 50 years of any pre-chosen date is high. This would transform the argument from 'it's unfalsifiable' to 'even if it were testable, the match isn't impressive.'
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a sentence to detail_evidence: 'Given SAA recurrence intervals of ~200–400 years and archaeomagnetic dating uncertainty of ±50–100 years, the 50-year gap between ~950 AD and the theological ~1000 AD target falls within noise — any of several recurrences could have been claimed as a match.' This strengthens the argument beyond 'unfalsifiable' to 'unfalsifiable AND statistically unimpressive even if it were testable.'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's own evolution notes (raw-text/06-evolution.txt) state: 'Completely failed to mathematically reconcile SAA splitting or predictive tellurics.' This is an internal admission that the dome's SAA physics doesn't work. If the dome model can't reconcile SAA splitting, it certainly can't claim to have predicted SAA formation timing. This self-admitted failure could be cited as additional evidence that WIN-023 is not a genuine prediction from dome physics.

kernel_of_truth.description: The ~950 AD date for the current SAA's reversed flux patch emergence is genuine, peer-reviewed paleomagnetic science (Campuzano et al. 2019, SHAWQ2k). The dome model correctly identifies a real geomagnetic event and correctly notes that the South Atlantic Anomaly is a significant and somewhat mysterious feature of Earth's magnetic field. The LLSVP-driven recurrence hypothesis, while well-supported, is still an area of active research — the exact mechanism for flux expulsion in this region is not fully settled.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying a real scientific finding doesn't constitute a prediction. The dome model found this date in published literature, not from its own geometry. More fundamentally, the dome provides no physical mechanism linking its parameters to the ~950 AD date — the 'prediction' is entirely post-hoc theological pattern-matching. The recurrence of SAA-like events at ~400, ~700, ~950, and ~1300 AD means cherry-picking the one closest to ~1000 AD is selection bias, not confirmation.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-023 finds the text substantially improved since Cycle 1. All three Cycle 1 issues — the wrong CHAOS-7 citation, the date-range conflation, and the soft retrodiction framing — have been addressed. The current text correctly cites Campuzano et al. 2019, Tarduno et al. 2015, and Hare et al. 2018 with DOI links; properly distinguishes recurrence episodes from the current SAA; and explicitly calls the dome's claim 'retrodiction dressed as prediction.'

The verdict of 'Unfalsifiable' remains correct and is well-supported by the evidence text. The theological overlay (linking ~950 AD to Revelation 20's 'Satan's little season ~1000 AD') is genuinely untestable — no observation could disprove a theological significance claim. The code_analysis tags are all accurate: hardcoded (static declaration only), post_hoc (adopted from 2019 literature), does not derive from dome (no formula connects dome parameters to a date), and does not relabel standard physics (adds theological interpretation rather than renaming the mechanism).

Three minor issues remain. First, the finding field is overlong for a summary table row — 269 characters where most entries use 50-120. This is a presentation issue, not a factual one. Second, there's a DOI discrepancy for Hare et al. 2018 between the current text and the Cycle 1 recommendation — one of the two DOIs may be incorrect. Third, the statistical recurrence argument (recurrence every 200-400 years makes a 50-year 'match' unremarkable) is mentioned in concept but not quantified. Adding a sentence about this would strengthen the argument from 'unfalsifiable' to 'unfalsifiable AND statistically trivial.'

A new stronger argument is available from the dome's own evolution notes, which admit failure to 'mathematically reconcile SAA splitting or predictive tellurics.' This self-admitted failure undermines the claim that WIN-023 represents a prediction from dome physics.

Overall confidence: 0.90 (up from 0.82 in Cycle 1). The text is now solid. The remaining issues are minor and the verdict is secure.

citations_verified: Campuzano et al. 2019 — DOI 10.1016/j.epsl.2019.01.046 — SHAWQ2k paper in EPSL, correct journal and topic
Tarduno et al. 2015 — DOI 10.1038/ncomms8865 — Nature Communications paper on SAA and inner core nucleation, correct
citations_failed: Hare et al. 2018 — DOI 10.1073/pnas.1720404115 in current text vs DOI 10.1073/pnas.1809197115 recommended in Cycle 1. One of these may be incorrect. Cannot verify without live DOI resolution. Flagged for manual check.
